title: Jihadist Killings Surge in Nigeria and DRC as Global Terror Deaths Drop
description: Terrorism fatalities jumped by 46% in Nigeria to 750, making it the fourth deadliest country in the Global Terrorism Index.
lede: Jihadist violence surged dramatically in Nigeria and the Democratic Republic of Congo in 2025, defying a broader global trend of declining terrorism deaths. Nigeria recorded a massive 46% increase in fatalities, solidifying its place as one of the world's most dangerous hotspots.

sections.heading: The Surge in Casualties
paragraphs: Nigeria saw terrorism-related deaths climb to 750 in 2025, up from 513 the previous year, securing its position as the fourth deadliest nation on the Global Terrorism Index behind Pakistan, Burkina Faso, and Niger.

In the Democratic Republic of Congo, the situation mirrored Nigeria's grim reality. Terrorism fatalities rose by nearly 28%, jumping from 365 to 467. This push elevated the DRC to eighth place on the global index, marking its worst ranking to date. The violence there was predominantly fueled by the Allied Democratic Forces (ADF), an affiliate of the Islamic State.
The violence hit a brutal peak in February when 162 people were massacred in Kwara state near the Benin Republic border, representing one of the deadliest single attacks in Nigeria's recent history.

sections.heading: A Fractured Security Landscape
paragraphs: Africa's most populous nation is fighting a multi-front security crisis. Established extremist factions like Boko Haram and its offshoots are continuously battling to seize territory, while ethnic militias and armed 'bandit' groups operate aggressively in the north and central regions. Compounding these threats is the emergence of newer terrorist elements such as the Lakurawa group.

Counter-insurgency operations are ramping up in response. On Wednesday, the Nigerian army reported repelling a coordinated Islamist assault on a military base in Borno state, killing at least 80 fighters. This followed a string of devastating suicide bombings in Maiduguri that left 23 dead and more than 100 wounded.

sections.heading: The Global Contrast
paragraphs: The escalating violence in these African nations stands in stark contrast to global trends. According to the Institute for Economics & Peace (IEP), total worldwide deaths from terrorism dropped by 28% to 5,582, and the number of overall attacks fell by nearly 22%.

Despite the global decline, the Sahel remains the epicenter of terrorism, accounting for over half of all terrorism-related deaths worldwide in 2025. Paradoxically, Burkina Faso saw a massive 50% drop in fatalities, suggesting a deliberate shift by al-Qaida affiliate JNIM to reduce civilian casualties and target military installations to consolidate power.
Meanwhile, the West witnessed a startling 280% increase in terrorism deaths, driven largely by youth radicalization and lone-wolf actors. The US recorded 28 fatalities, its highest figure since 2019.

sections.heading: Shifting Tactics and Drone Warfare
paragraphs: Jihadist groups across the region are exhibiting increasing sophistication. The tactical shift by groups like JNIM indicates a move from civilian massacres to coordinated assaults on high-value military and political targets, a 'value versus vulnerability' strategy designed to secure territorial gains.

This modernization is highlighted by the growing use of drones. JNIM has deployed drones in over 100 incidents across the Sahel over the last three years. The Islamic State West Africa Province (ISWAP) has also engaged in drone warfare, utilizing them for both direct attacks and crucial surveillance for ground offensives.
